images.caption:
Figure 2:(a) Two failure modes that motivate ZPPO. In the small-student regime, fitting the student to a much larger teacher’s logits is brittle; on the RL side, injecting a teacher response into the policy gradient as if it were on-policy introduces drift.
(b) BCQ recovers a learning signal on hard questions by pairing a correct teacher response with a wrong student response as anonymized in-prompt candidates, asking the student to reason about which one is correct.
(c) NCQ aggregates the student’s own wrong rollouts into a single prompt, surfacing shared error patterns.

Figure 3:Overview of ZPPO.(a) Hard questions (mean rollout accuracy below half) are admitted to the prompt replay buffer.
(b) BCQ pairs one correct teacher response with one wrong student response.
(c) NCQ aggregates the student’s wrong rollouts into a single prompt.
(d) Integrated batch drives the policy gradient update with RL Recipe.
Figure 4:Cumulative graduate counts (graduated / admitted==ratio) for ZPPO vs. GRPO†at 2B by entry rollout accuracy at admission;†denotes augmentation with the prompt replay buffer.

Figure 5:(a) Composition of a single training rollout batch over the full run into Easy (r¯x≥0.5\bar{r}_{x}\!\geq\!0.5), BCQ, and NCQ shares, per student scale. (b) Cumulative graduation vs. FIFO-eviction ratio of the prompt replay buffer.

Figure 6:RL recipe ablation at 2B on the 10 LLM (a, d), 16 VLM (b, e), and 5 Video (c, f) benchmarks.(Top, a–c)Iterations per stepI∈{1,4,8,16}I\!\in\!\{1,4,8,16\}.(Bottom, d–f)Batch-level advantage normalization:No norm,Norm w/o Zero(ZPPO default), andNorm w/ Zero. Dashed gray line marks original 2B.

Figure 20:Replay-buffer composition by entry rollout accuracy at admission, over training, for ZPPO at every student scale. Each panel stacks the four admission bins so the total band height is the buffer occupancy.

Figure 21:(left)Total number of graduations per scale, broken down by entry rollout accuracy at admission (0%,12.512.5%,2525%,37.537.5%).(right)Per-scale composition of graduates. Higher bars on the leftmost colors at larger scales mean “ZPPO graduates a larger absolute number of the hardest entries (admitted at0%) at larger scales”, not that hardest entries become a smaller share of the buffer.

Figure 22:Training dynamics of Qwen3.5-2B + ZPPO vs. Qwen3.5-2B + GRPO†. Left: student rollout accuracy. Center: fraction of student rollouts whose group-relative advantage is positive. Right: actor Shannon entropy of the policy distribution.

Figure 23:Replay-buffer fate of completed entries (graduated vs. FIFO-evicted) at22B for ZPPO and GRPO†. ZPPO almost doubles the graduation rate (26.626.6% vs.14.314.3%) and proportionally cuts evictions, on the same buffer with the same admission/graduation thresholds.

Figure 24:(left two panels)Replay graduations partitioned by entry rollout accuracy at admission, for GRPO†vs. ZPPO at22B (counts and per-model composition).(right two panels)Per-bucket fate decomposition by number of re-sample attempts: ZPPO converts the “improved (non-grad)” (light green) bucket into “graduated” (dark green) much more aggressively than GRPO†.

Figure 25:ZPPO training dynamics across all four student scales.(top row)policy gradient loss, BCQ-prompt accuracy, NCQ-prompt accuracy.(bottom row)fraction of positive advantages, student rollout accuracy, actor Shannon entropy.

Figure 26:Visual counting,22B student.Worked BCQ/NCQ example on a hard ZPPO-7777K question that the22B student fails on every plain rollout (r¯x=0\bar{r}_{x}\!=\!0).(a) BCQ: one teacher-correct and one student-wrong trace, teacher-rewritten to length parity, anonymized, and randomly shuffled.(b) NCQ: all of the student’s wrong rollouts listed as negatives, with the teacher trace excluded from the prompt. Both branches recover a correct rollout. Figs.27–30extend the same template to four other (scale, domain, outcome) cells.

Figure 27:Visual counting,44B student.Worked BCQ/NCQ example on a hard ZPPO-7777K free-form counting question that the44B student fails on every plain rollout (r¯x=0\bar{r}_{x}\!=\!0).(a) BCQcontrasts the under-counting trace against one that reads the alternating colour pattern;(b) NCQexcludes the teacher trace and tells the student that the consensus answer (3) is wrong. Both branches recover the correct count of 4.

Figure 28:Scene QA,22B student.Worked BCQ/NCQ example on a hard ZPPO-7777K question that the22B student fails on every plain rollout (r¯x=0\bar{r}_{x}\!=\!0) by misidentifying the salient attachment (six rollouts pick the pocket flap, two pick buttons).(a) BCQcontrasts the misidentification trace against the braided-cord trace;(b) NCQconfronts the student with both of its own wrong answer modes (BandC); after ruling them out, the student matches the chest-knot configuration described in the listed traces to the remaining optionD. Both branches recover the correct answer.

Figure 29:Chart-reading,99B student.Worked BCQ/NCQ example on an open-ended numeric ZPPO-7777K question that the99B student plain-fails (r¯x=0\bar{r}_{x}\!=\!0) by down-snapping the unlabelledy=500y\!=\!500atx=1x\!=\!1to the labelled400400-row.(a) BCQ: contrasts that400400-row chain against one anchored on exact-intersection grid points and the arrowhead’s terminating position.(b) NCQ: with the teacher trace excluded, the student re-derives500500by positional grounding – elimination is unavailable on an open-ended numeric answer space.

Figure 30:Visual counting,0.80.8B student – BCQ✓\boldsymbol{\checkmark}/ NCQ×\boldsymbol{\times}.The0.80.8B student plain-fails (r¯x=0\bar{r}_{x}\!=\!0) by overlooking a cluster of straws on the right edge.(a) BCQ: presented with a positionally-grounded compressed alternative (right-edge cluster, cylindrical/white, count of three), the student commits to it because that localized claim is checkable, and recovers33.(b) NCQ: with the teacher trace removed and shown88instances of its own “no straws” answer plus the instruction that0is wrong, the student fails to override its own consensus and repeats0\boxed{0}on every rollout – the qualitative shape of the0.80.8B match-neg row in Tab.17.
